README.title: How to use this vault
README.html:
How to use this vault

1. Drop this entire Bloom Energy folder inside your Obsidian vault (or open this folder as its own vault).

2. Open 00 Project Home.md — that's your map of content.

3. Make sure these core community plugins are enabled in Obsidian: Templater, Dataview, Tag Wrangler.

4. Use the templates in 06 Templates/ when creating new notes (Templater plugin).

5. Update 07 Daily/ with a note every research session.

Folder structure

  • 01 Sources/ — one note per primary source (10-K, press release, news article). Each has a URL.
  • 02 Atomic Notes/ — one note per fact or claim. Always linked to its source.
  • 03 Drafts/ — in-progress deliverables. Built from atomic notes via wiki links.
  • 04 Final Deliverables/ — submitted versions.
  • 05 Questions/ — open research questions. Triage weekly.
  • 06 Templates/ — note templates for Templater plugin.
  • 07 Daily/ — daily research log.

Working with Claude

Connect Claude to this vault via the Obsidian MCP connector. Once connected, Claude can read all notes and create new ones in the right folders. Useful prompts:

  • "Read all atomic notes tagged #moat and draft a 1-page competitive summary."
  • "Look at my drafts folder and tell me what facts I have not yet cited."
  • "Read all #bear notes and stress-test my investment thesis."
  • "Summarize what I learned this week from the daily notes."

01 Sources/Bloom Energy Server with Heat.title: Bloom Energy Server with Heat Capture — Brochure (2024)
01 Sources/Bloom Energy Server with Heat.path: 01 Sources/Bloom Energy Server with Heat Capture Brochure 2024.md
01 Sources/Bloom Energy Server with Heat.html:
Bloom Energy Server with Heat Capture — Brochure (2024)

Primary product brochure for the Energy Server with Heat Capture, Bloom's Combined Heat and Power (CHP) configuration. The exhaust adapters bolt onto the back of the Power Modules and harness the >350°C exhaust at two connection points for integration with heat-recovery equipment. Bloom offers an installation guide and full turnkey CHP solutions.

Headline efficiency (1 MW installation, lifetime average)

| Metric | Value |

|---|---|

| Electrical efficiency | 54% |

| Heat recovery efficiency | >36% |

| Total CHP efficiency | >90% |

| Exhaust temperature | 356–384 °C (avg ~367 °C) |

All values modeled, ±5% vs. field data; assume pipeline natural gas at ISO conditions.

Three exhaust-heat applications

  • Chilled water (cooling) — exhaust drives an absorption chiller / Vapor Absorption Machine (VAM) to make chilled water, displacing electric chillers. The data-center use. (1 MW yields 175–200 tons of cooling.)
  • Steam generation — 5–15 barg, dry-saturated or superheated, for industrial/pharma/electronics.
  • Hot water — 13,000–16,500 kg/h @ 80 °C for food processing, space heating, facility hot water.

A condensing economizer in series adds a minimum +5% efficiency boost. Without CHP, the facility would have burned extra gas or drawn extra electricity for the same heating/cooling.

Notes

The >90% figure is combined efficiency (electricity + captured, used heat), realized only where a thermal load sits next to the box. On gas with heat vented, the box is a 54% machine. The block size is 325 kW, scalable to hundreds of MW; low CO₂ footprint, negligible NOx/SOx, no particulates.

Key takeaways

  • Brookfield increases its financing framework for Bloom-powered AI infrastructure projects from $5B to $25B — a fivefold expansion in under 9 months (original deal Oct 13, 2025).
  • Funding comes from Brookfield's dedicated AI Infrastructure Fund (launched Nov 2025, $100B deployment target across AI factories, power, compute, and capital partnerships).
  • Framing: "a new model for AI factories that integrates power, compute, data center infrastructure, and capital from the outset" — Brookfield pitches "electrons to tokens" end-to-end delivery.
  • Bloom CCO Aman Joshi: original deal was "the first phase of a much larger vision"; expansion "reflects the momentum we are seeing in the market, as evidenced by recently announced large-scale deals."
  • Brookfield: >$1T AUM; >$100B already invested in digital infrastructure and clean power.
  • Stock jumped ~10% on the announcement (TIKR/Motley Fool coverage).

Analytical notes (ours)

  • This is a financing framework, not firm product orders — same caveat as the ~$20B backlog: it converts as projects are actually built. But it materially de-risks the funding side of deployment.
  • Sharpens the existing revenue-quality question: the related-party/affiliate channel (Brookfield JVs) that was ~half of Q1 2026 revenue now has 5× more capital behind it. Bull: massive validated demand + capital. Bear: even more of Bloom's growth flows through a partner whose projects Bloom helps finance, with the margin split still undisclosed.

01 Sources/Hunterbrook Short Report and.html:
Hunterbrook Short Report ("Bloom's Big Lie") and Bloom's 8-K Rebuttal (July 8–9, 2026)

The allegations (Hunterbrook Media, July 8, 2026)

Hunterbrook disclosed it may hold positions benefiting from a BE decline. Three claims:

1. China-dependent scandium supply. Using trade data, corporate filings, satellite imagery, and interviews, Hunterbrook traced supply routes it says contradict management's repeated statements that Bloom has no China-dependent supply chain.

2. Scandium scarcity math. Bloom's 5 GW production ambition would need ~220 tons of scandium oxide/year vs projected global supply of ~240 tons — nearly the entire world market.

3. Backlog vs firm orders. Challenged the unaudited ~$20B backlog against audited GAAP remaining performance obligations of roughly $492M (updated from the ~$441M we cite from Q1).

Stock fell ~6% on publication.

Bloom's response (8-K, Item 7.01, July 9, 2026)

  • Categorically rejects claims about financial results and accounting as "false and misleading"; stands behind audited statements (10-K filed Feb 9, 2026; 10-Q filed Apr 29, 2026).
  • Rejects scandium conclusions: sufficient scandium oxide for current demand and backlog; supply and future scaling not dependent on China; claims supply-chain visibility supporting 25 GW/year of fuel cell production.
  • Stock recovered ~6% on the rebuttal; law-firm "securities fraud investigations" announced (standard post-short-report ambulance chasing, but worth tracking).

Analytical notes (ours)

  • This is the 2019 Hindenburg playbook re-run for the AI era — and it targets exactly the two soft spots our Risks section already flags: backlog vs RPO and supply-chain concentration (we flagged MTAR as sole hot-box supplier and FEOC/scandium ITC exposure before this report).
  • The 25 GW/yr visibility claim is new and aggressive (prior public figure: 2 GW capacity by end-2026, stretchable to 5 GW). Treat as management assertion, unverified.
  • Note the RPO figure moved: ~$441M (Q1 10-Q as we recorded) → ~$492M per Hunterbrook's citation. Verify against the Q1 10-Q / Q2 10-Q when filed.
  • Market took Bloom's side within a day — but the report gives the bear case a fresh, dated, citable source.
  • Site sections affected: Recent news (two items: report + rebuttal), Risks (degradation/dilution card or a new supply-chain card), possibly Competition "watch."

Per Kshana research (verify): Bloom is "safe harboring" the 30% Investment Tax Credit for deployments through 2028, locking credit eligibility (via construction-start / equipment-procurement rules) against a ~$12–15B gross product-revenue pipeline through 2028. 02 Atomic Notes/Heat Capture CHP raises.title: Heat Capture (CHP) reuses the >350°C exhaust to lift total efficiency from 54% to >90%
02 Atomic Notes/Heat Capture CHP raises.path: 02 Atomic Notes/Heat Capture CHP raises total efficiency above 90 percent.md
02 Atomic Notes/Heat Capture CHP raises.html:
Heat Capture (CHP) reuses the >350°C exhaust to lift total efficiency from 54% to >90%

The Energy Server with Heat Capture — a productized configuration Bloom has been rolling out — bolts exhaust adapters onto the Power Modules to harness exhaust at 356–384°C. Reusing that heat lifts the system from 54% electrical to >36% heat recovery to >90% total CHP efficiency [R] (modeled ±5%).

The three uses are hot water, steam, and chilled water — the latter via an absorption chiller (VAM), which is the data-center use: it displaces electric chillers and improves PUE (Bloom claims a 10–15% gain), a second dollar saving on top of fuel. Bloom also lists additional electricity generation among possible waste-heat uses, but for data centers the value is cooling, not added power.

Caveats: the >90% figure only lands where a thermal load sits next to the box; the chiller is added capital; the heat figures are modeled to ±5%, not field-validated; >350°C is high-grade for cooling/steam but well short of turbine-grade. Treat it as a win-rate input, not a separate revenue line.

02 Atomic Notes/LEGO block architecture.title: Bloom's LEGO block architecture: more blocks = more reliability (large projects MORE reliable than small)
02 Atomic Notes/LEGO block architecture.path: 02 Atomic Notes/LEGO block architecture more blocks more reliability.md
02 Atomic Notes/LEGO block architecture.html:
Bloom's LEGO block architecture: more blocks = more reliability (large projects MORE reliable than small)

Ben Kallo (Baird) asked how customers got comfortable with larger Bloom projects. KR Sridhar: 'The boxes do not know whether they are sitting along with five other boxes or 5,000 other boxes. Nothing in our scaling has scaling risk. In fact, because these are hard swappable LEGO blocks, the more LEGO blocks you have, the more reliable our system gets. So large block power becomes a lot more reliable than small block power.'

This is a meaningful counterintuitive point. Adding redundant modular units increases system availability through fault tolerance (N+1 redundancy compounds). A 100 MW Bloom install is statistically MORE reliable than a 10 MW install, contrary to typical industrial machinery scaling.

This directly answers the bear concern that 'we haven't seen Bloom at gigawatt scale.' Scaling is just adding LEGO blocks; no engineering risk.

Book-and-ship matters for two reasons. (1) It's a competitive advantage when other vendors miss their promises and customers come to Bloom for rescue. (2) It implies Bloom can capture sudden demand that doesn't appear in any backlog. KR expects this to continue at double-digit percentages.

Unsubsidized Bloom system capex runs ~$6,640-$8,000/kW. The 30% §48E ITC delivers roughly $2,250/kW of capex relief on a ~$7,500/kW system, which works out to ~3¢/kWh levelized reduction in delivered electricity cost.

02 Atomic Notes/Backlog 20B vs product R.title: Only ~$441M of the ~$20B "backlog" is GAAP RPO — the rest is framework pipeline, not firm obligation
02 Atomic Notes/Backlog 20B vs product R.path: 02 Atomic Notes/Backlog 20B vs product RPO 441M reconciliation.md
02 Atomic Notes/Backlog 20B vs product R.html:
Only ~$441M of the ~$20B "backlog" is GAAP RPO — the rest is framework pipeline, not firm obligation

Per Kshana research (verify vs 10-Q): the ~$20B backlog and the ~$441M product Remaining Performance Obligation (RPO) measure very different things. RPO (ASC 606) counts only firm, non-cancellable product commitments. The gap is driven by:

  • Service (~$14B): excluded via the "right-to-invoice" practical expedient (recurring monthly maintenance), despite the 100% attach rate.
  • Product (~$6B): includes anticipated ITC and incentives, which aren't customer "unearned revenue" until a system is commissioned.
  • Framework deals (Oracle/AEP/AWS): secure manufacturing slots and "pipeline velocity" but only convert to RPO as specific POs are executed.

Takeaway: the $20B is opportunity/pipeline, not contracted revenue — backlog-to-RPO conversion is "lumpy." This directly answers the backlog-quality question (framework ceiling ≠ firm order book).

⚠ Research-derived; confirm the exact RPO figure and composition against the 10-Q.

Brookfield-JV product margin runs an estimated 400–600 bps below peak third-party sales

Per Kshana research (estimate, not disclosed): sales through the Brookfield "AI Fund" framework carry a product gross margin roughly 400–600 basis points below peak arm's-length third-party sales — the delta reflecting the financing-framework/shared-project economics versus the "time-to-power" premium third parties pay.

This is the first quantified answer to the previously-undisclosed related-vs-third-party margin split (our #1 open risk), but it is an analyst reconstruction, not a filing line item. Kshana argues the lower gross margin is offset by operating leverage (manufacturing overhead absorption) — Q1 2026 operating margin reportedly reached 17.3% (+~1,300 bps). Bloom's stated 2026 target is a 34% non-GAAP gross margin.

⚠ Estimate only; Bloom does NOT disclose the margin split. Treat as a working assumption to pressure-test, not fact.

02 Atomic Notes/MTAR Technologies single.title: MTAR Technologies (India) is a single-source "Hot Box" supplier — a supply-chain single point of failure
02 Atomic Notes/MTAR Technologies single.path: 02 Atomic Notes/MTAR Technologies single-source Hot Box supplier.md
02 Atomic Notes/MTAR Technologies single.html:
MTAR Technologies (India) is a single-source "Hot Box" supplier — a supply-chain single point of failure

Per Kshana research (verify): the Hot Box — the core high-temperature assembly of the Bloom Energy Server — is built by MTAR Technologies in India. India is not a covered nation (so it's FEOC-clean), but the concentration is a single-source dependency: the "copy exact" architecture requires MTAR to match Bloom's cadence precisely, so any geopolitical or operational disruption at MTAR would bottleneck Bloom's entire ramp.

This is a distinct risk from FEOC exposure: operational/concentration risk on a critical component. MTAR has supplied Bloom's hot boxes (and electrolyzer units) for 9+ years as sole supplier, and is scaling capacity from 8,000 units/yr → 12,000 (FY26) → 20,000 (FY27) → 30,000 (FY28), with Bloom co-investing in that expansion — so the dependency is deep and deliberate, and MTAR's ability to scale in lockstep gates Bloom's delivery timelines.

✓ CONFIRMED via India trade/analyst coverage (sole hot-box supplier, capacity ramp, Bloom investment). Sources: T&D India · Hydrogen Fuel News

02 Atomic Notes/800V DC water through st.title: 800V DC 'water through a straw' analogy: from 13 kW CPU racks to 650-1300 kW Rubin AI racks
02 Atomic Notes/800V DC water through st.path: 02 Atomic Notes/800V DC water through straw analogy CPU to Rubin.md
02 Atomic Notes/800V DC water through st.html:
800V DC 'water through a straw' analogy: from 13 kW CPU racks to 650-1300 kW Rubin AI racks

KR Sridhar's most elegant 800V DC explanation (Q3 2025 call): 'Power coming into the rack is like a hungry human being drinking water through a straw. The 48V DC was sufficient when CPU racks were 13 kW. They put a lot of band-aids on it to make sure Blackwell chips that come somewhere near the 130 kW can handle it. With a straw. Guess what? The Rubin chips and going forward are going to be five times that to 10 times that.'

Rack power trajectory:

  • Legacy CPU: 13 kW
  • Nvidia Blackwell: ~130 kW (10x)
  • Nvidia Rubin and beyond: 650-1300 kW (5-10x Blackwell)

To deliver that much power through the same physical rack space, voltage MUST go up (laws of physics). 800V DC is the only viable answer. Bloom is the only on-site solution that produces 800V DC natively because it was architected for DC from day one in 2000.

02 Atomic Notes/7 AI ecosystem channels.path: 02 Atomic Notes/7 AI ecosystem channels framework.md
02 Atomic Notes/7 AI ecosystem channels.html:
Bloom's AI strategy is built on 7 distinct ecosystem channels, each with a lighthouse customer

On the Q3 2025 call, KR Sridhar laid out an explicit framework: 'We are embedded in seven distinct AI ecosystem channels. In each channel, we have secured a lighthouse customer and have robust pipelines.'

The 7 channels:

1. Hyperscalers (Oracle - delivered in 55 days vs 90-day promise)

2. Electricity providers (AEP gigawatt deal, powering AWS)

3. Gas providers (first deal signed, hyperscaler to be announced)

4. Colocation providers (Equinix 100+ MW across multiple states)

5. Neo Clouds (CoreWeave at Illinois high-performance data center)

6. Data center developers (filing permits, public records)

7. Infrastructure owners (Brookfield $5B inaugural investment)

This is the most important strategic framing in any 2025 disclosure. Bloom is not just selling to AI; they have 7 different sales motions running in parallel, each with its own lighthouse and pipeline. Significantly de-risks the 'all-eggs-in-Oracle' narrative.

02 Atomic Notes/SOFC concentrated exhaus.title: Bloom's concentrated, combustion-free exhaust enables ~95% purity carbon capture cheaply
02 Atomic Notes/SOFC concentrated exhaus.path: 02 Atomic Notes/SOFC concentrated exhaust enables 95 percent purity carbon capture.md
02 Atomic Notes/SOFC concentrated exhaus.html:
Bloom's concentrated, combustion-free exhaust enables ~95% purity carbon capture cheaply

Because the SOFC reaction is electrochemical (no combustion), its exhaust is ~10× the CO₂ concentration of a gas turbine and free of NOx/SOx/particulates — which makes capture cheaper and lets Bloom isolate a CO₂ stream of ~95% purity for use or sequestration (Bloom carbon-capture page). The Chart Industries JV targets this against the $85/ton capture credit ($180/ton if pulled from air). Paired with biogas, the combination can deliver negative-carbon power.

This is subsidy-linked terminal-value optionality, not a near-term revenue driver — alongside the SOEC electrolyzer (hydrogen), it sits in terminal value rather than the base forecast.

02 Atomic Notes/3 financing models CapEx.title: Bloom has 3 go-to-market financing models: CapEx (direct), PPA, and Managed Services (legacy/wind-down)

02 Atomic Notes/3 financing models CapEx.html:
Bloom has 3 go-to-market financing models: CapEx (direct), PPA, and Managed Services (legacy/wind-down)

Maciej Kurzymski explained the financing structures on the Q3 2025 call:

1. CapEx (direct sale): Customer writes a big check, effectively prepaying for years of electricity. Some quarter-to-quarter activity.

2. PPA financing: The majority of transactions. Bloom or partner finances; customer pays per kWh.

3. Managed Services (sale-leaseback): 'We have not done managed service transactions in quite some time. We do not expect to do those going forward.'

The explicit retirement of Managed Services confirms Bloom's strategic exit from the Electricity-segment own-the-box model. Going forward = mostly PPA + some CapEx. This matters for revenue mix forecasting.

02 Atomic Notes/54 percent lifetime elec.title: 54% is Bloom's honest lifetime electrical efficiency on natural gas
02 Atomic Notes/54 percent lifetime elec.path: 02 Atomic Notes/54 percent lifetime electrical efficiency on gas.md
02 Atomic Notes/54 percent lifetime elec.html:
54% is Bloom's honest lifetime electrical efficiency on natural gas

Three efficiency figures circulate and measure different things; the honest, real-world number on gas is 54% lifetime average electrical efficiency (degradation included), per Bloom's own Heat Capture brochure [R].

  • 54% — lifetime average electrical efficiency on natural gas
  • 60% — electrical efficiency on 100% hydrogen at the R&D facility
  • 90% — combined electricity plus captured, used heat (CHP) — not the electrical figure, and not applicable to data centers unless the heat is used

The trap is stacking 90% against 54%. On gas with the heat vented, the box is a 54% machine. (The datasheet's 53–65% is the Server 6.5 nameplate range; 54% is the de-rated lifetime figure.) At 54%, the box needs 6.32 MMBtu of gas per MWh.

02 Atomic Notes/10-15 year figure is ser.title: The "10–15 years" figure is the service-commitment length, not the physical stack life
02 Atomic Notes/10-15 year figure is ser.path: 02 Atomic Notes/10-15 year figure is service commitment not stack life.md
02 Atomic Notes/10-15 year figure is ser.html:
The "10–15 years" figure is the service-commitment length, not the physical stack life

Correction to a common conflation (the Kshana research made it): the 10–15 years (up to 20) cited for data-center deals is the service-contract commitment, not how long a fuel-cell stack physically lasts. Actual stack life is ~4.8–5.2 years for 2016–18 vintages — improving ~2.5× vs first-generation — and stacks are swapped on-site in under an hour when they degrade. Over a 10–20-year contract a customer therefore pays for multiple stack replacements, which is exactly what makes service a recurring annuity.

So "10–15-year stack life" (Kshana) is misleading; the accurate read is a 10–20-year service relationship built on ~5-year replaceable stacks. The bull point still holds — steady data-center duty is easier on stacks than cycling, and service margin has inflected positive — but the long number is a contract term, not a durability spec.

✓ CONFIRMED via Bloom longevity disclosures (4.8/5.2-yr vintages, 2.5× improvement, <1-hr swap; 10–20-yr service commitments). Sources: Bloom – longevity

02 Atomic Notes/Scandia-stabilized zirco.title: Scandia-stabilized zirconia (scandium) is Bloom's most FEOC-sensitive input
02 Atomic Notes/Scandia-stabilized zirco.path: 02 Atomic Notes/Scandia-stabilized zirconia scandium most FEOC-sensitive input.md
02 Atomic Notes/Scandia-stabilized zirco.html:
Scandia-stabilized zirconia (scandium) is Bloom's most FEOC-sensitive input

Per Kshana research (verify): Bloom's SOFC electrolyte uses Scandia-Stabilized Zirconia (ScSZ) / scandia-ceria-stabilized zirconia for higher conductivity at lower temperature — making scandium the most FEOC-sensitive material in the bill of materials. A large share of global scandium historically comes from Soviet-era stockpiles or is processed in China (a "covered nation"), so even with domestic final assembly, the mineral's upstream "material assistance" can jeopardize customers' 30% ITC.

The regulatory framework is confirmed (OBBB + Bipartisan Policy Center analysis): the Material Assistance Cost Ratio (MACR) tests the % of component value sourced from non-Prohibited Foreign Entities (must exceed a threshold to qualify). A "Foreign-Influenced Entity" is triggered at 25% ownership / a board seat / 40% aggregate / 15% of debt. IRS safe-harbor tables are due end-2026; Treasury updates critical-mineral thresholds by Dec 31, 2027; contracts finalized before June 16, 2025 (in service before Jan 1, 2030) are grandfathered.

◑ PARTIAL: the FEOC/MACR framework and dates are confirmed (Bipartisan Policy Center · DOE FEOC guidance). Bloom's specific scandium sourcing and its measured MACR are NOT publicly confirmed — the load-bearing unknown; verify via filings/expert channels.

Combined Heat and Power pushes total system efficiency above 90%

Bloom's Integrated Heat Capture / CHP product captures the 800C+ exhaust heat and reuses it for hot water, building heat, process steam, or chilled water via absorption chillers. Combined electrical + thermal efficiency rises from ~54% (electricity only) to over 90%.

Why it matters: in data center deployments, absorption-chiller cooling can drive a 10-15% PUE improvement, turning the worst-case loss (wasted heat) into a second product.

KR Sridhar: 'Bloom, and only Bloom, natively produces 800 volts DC today. No Band-Aids, or adapters needed.'

Gas turbines, reciprocating engines, and the grid all deliver AC at high voltage (35-69 kV). To serve an 800V DC AI rack, they require transformers, rectifiers, and power conditioning that add cost, reduce reliability, and increase losses. Bloom's solid-state architecture skips all of this.

Bloom's DC architecture has shipped in every server for the last 15 years

KR Sridhar: 'We built an architecture where we can feed these straws appropriately right at that 800 volts and we decided every unit we have shipped for the last 15 years has that. But after that, we have one other box that takes that DC and makes it into AC and provides it because we were making color TV images. The world was only consuming black and white.'

The 'color TV / black and white' analogy: Bloom has been generating native DC and converting it to AC for the AC-only legacy data center market. Going forward, Bloom can simply remove the DC-to-AC inverter and ship native 800V DC. The capability has been there for 15 years and is now finally what the market wants.

For retrofit potential: previously shipped servers can be modified to remove the inverter, so Bloom's existing installed base is forward-compatible.

02 Atomic Notes/All-in cost 121 per MWh.title: Bloom's all-in cost is ~$121/MWh (~$95 with the 30% ITC); fuel is only $22–32 of it
02 Atomic Notes/All-in cost 121 per MWh.path: 02 Atomic Notes/All-in cost 121 per MWh fuel slice small.md
02 Atomic Notes/All-in cost 121 per MWh.html:
Bloom's all-in cost is ~$121/MWh (~$95 with the 30% ITC); fuel is only $22–32 of it

Bloom's levelized all-in delivered cost is roughly $121/MWh unsubsidized, or ~$95/MWh with the 30% ITC [D]. The fuel slice is only $22–32/MWh (6.32 MMBtu/MWh at 54% efficiency × $3.50–5.00/MMBtu); the box and stack-replacement service are the other four-fifths.

Implication: higher efficiency shrinks only the fuel slice. Bloom undercuts an inefficient on-site genset on fuel (a 40% genset burns 8.53 MMBtu/MWh) yet still costs more than grid power overall. Bloom sells speed and reliability, not commodity price. Hydrogen makes the fuel line ~10× worse (~$200/MWh at $4–6/kg).

05 Questions/Open Questions — Answered June 2026

05 Questions/Open Questions \u2014 Answe.title: Open Questions — Answered (research pass, June 2026)
05 Questions/Open Questions \u2014 Answe.html:
Open Questions — Answered (research pass, June 2026)

Web research answering the eleven open questions. Each entry: verdict, key evidence, what stays non-public, and sources. Confidence noted. Academic use; not investment advice.

Q1 · Pricing discipline when gas turbines catch up (2028–2029)?

Verdict (med-high): The squeeze is real but back-half-decade, not imminent. Turbine lead times are near their ~5-year peak now and only begin compressing around 2027, with real relief 2029–2030. Bloom's speed (12–18-mo deploy), native DC, and double-digit annual cost-downs should hold pricing through ~2028; risk builds 2029–2030 as turbine surplus and falling turbine prices arrive together.

  • GE Vernova ~80 GW backlog, effectively sold out through 2030; Siemens Energy €136B backlog; Mitsubishi turbines ordered now deliver 2028–2030.
  • Jefferies: equipment capacity 19 GW (2028) → 49 (2029) → 76 (2030); lead times "start dropping by 2027." Turbine prices +195% since 2019 (~$600/kW) — currently helping Bloom.
  • _Non-public:_ Bloom's per-kW COGS trajectory; confidential turbine ASPs.
  • Sources: Utility Dive – GE Vernova · Utility Dive – bottleneck easing · Wood Mackenzie – turbine prices

Q4 · On-site power: permanent layer or temporary bridge?

Verdict (med): Hyperscalers explicitly call gas/fuel cells a "bridge" to grid + nuclear — but the bridge runs well into the 2030s and the endpoint is undefined, so on-site DG is effectively a structural layer for this decade and likely persists as permanent resilience/peaking capacity.

  • SMRs realistically 2028–2030 for first small units, bulk 2031–2035 (Kairos/Google ~2030; Oklo ~2027–28, microreactor scale).
  • Grid interconnection waits average ~5 years; FERC Order 2023 + June 2026 large-load orders aim to help but near-term relief doubted.
  • Microsoft LOI 1.4 GW behind-the-meter gas; Oracle hedges Bloom SOFC + VoltaGrid gas.
  • _Non-public:_ how much on-site capacity hyperscalers retire once grid/nuclear arrive.
  • Sources: Latitude Media · FERC RM26-4 · WWT – SMRs

Q11 · Is the 800V DC transition real, and is Bloom ahead?

Verdict (high on direction): Real, physics-driven, NVIDIA-forced. Pilots 2026, broad adoption 2027–2028 with the Kyber/Rubin Ultra generation. Bloom is genuinely ahead — its cells natively output DC and (per Bloom) no rival natively produces 800V DC today — but it's a first-mover edge, not a patent moat: AC→800VDC "sidecars" (Vertiv, Schneider) let turbine/grid power reach 800V racks too.

  • NVIDIA standardizing 800 VDC, first in Kyber racks (2027, ~600 kW/rack); moves >150% more power through the same copper. Vertiv 800V systems shipping H2 2026.
  • Adoption favors greenfield builds (brownfield AC retrofit costly) — good for Bloom's new-build pairing. UBS raised PT $170→$251 on 800VDC readiness.
  • _Non-public:_ system-level efficiency gain of native-DC vs sidecar; whether hyperscalers pay a premium for native DC.
  • Sources: NVIDIA developer blog · SemiAnalysis · DCD – 1MW racks

Q6 · Why is installation gross margin still ~−35%?

Verdict: A small, lumpy, non-strategic line — a mix/scale artifact, not a deliberate loss-leader. Install is <4% of revenue; the −35% reflects a collapsing install-revenue base against relatively fixed costs as more installs move to Brookfield-/customer-financed EPC.

  • Q1'26 install revenue $25.9M vs cost $35.1M = −$9.1M (−35.3%). Trend: Q1'25 +1.0% → Q4'25 −10.7% → Q1'26 −35.3%. Product margin 34.3%, service +13.3%, consolidated 30.0% — install drag immaterial.
  • Industrial EPC/install typically low-single-digit to ~10% GM, so structurally negative isn't the norm; it's Bloom's portfolio choice. No explicit "break-even at scale" guidance.
  • Sources: Q1 2026 results (SEC) · Q1 2026 transcript

Q7 · Can margins survive a hyperscaler renegotiation?

Verdict (med): Deals are multi-year master agreements with volume frameworks, but product largely ships order-by-order off those frameworks, so pricing is periodically reset, not locked for life. Bloom keeps more pricing power than a commodity supplier (sole-source, grid-independent, 100%-attached service annuity), but concentration risk is real and rising.

  • Oracle: MSA up to 2.8 GW (only ~1.2 GW contracted) — a ceiling, not full take-or-pay; Oracle got a ~$400M Bloom warrant (Bloom conceded economics). AEP: $2.65B / 20-yr / ~1 GW (closest to locked). SK: historically take-or-pay with "premium pricing."
  • Top-3 customers ≈ 68% of FY2025 revenue (43/13/12), largest is related-party.
  • _Non-public:_ per-watt pricing, MFN/cancellation clauses, gross margin by customer cohort.
  • Sources: CNBC – Oracle/warrant · FY2025 10-K (SEC)

Q9 · Real quality of revenue given ~50% related-party?

Verdict (high on facts): Bloom states Brookfield-JV sales are "transacted at arms-length and prevailing market terms" and it defers intra-entity profit — so revenue isn't obviously inflated. But quality is muddied: ~44–50% routes through JVs Bloom co-owns and finances, and Bloom books equity-method losses on them.

  • Q1'26: related-party $373.3M vs $377.8M arm's-length. FY2025: $892.0M of $2,024.0M ≈ 44%. Pricing = "cost-plus margin." Equity-method loss −$17.0M Q1'26 (−$40.4M FY2025); deferred-profit liability $22.8M.
  • SK ecoplant ceased to be a related party July 10, 2025 — so related-party is now essentially the Brookfield Fund JVs.
  • _Non-public:_ gross-margin split related vs third-party; end-customer economics inside the JVs; independent verification of "market terms." This is the legitimate red flag to discount for.
  • Sources: Q1 2026 results (SEC) · FY2025 10-K (SEC)

Q8 · Will stack life hold under heavy AI duty cycles?

Verdict (med-high on direction): Continuous, steady-state data-center loads are likely neutral-to-favorable vs the on/off cycling that historically drives SOFC degradation — which structurally supports the thesis. Real risk is elevated current density at near-100% utilization, and that Bloom's multi-decade fade at data-center duty is still unproven at scale.

  • Literature: thermal cycling dominates degradation (voltage decay 8.8% at 1°C/min vs 19.1% at 5°C/min); higher current density compounds fade.
  • Bloom MTTR improved 1.9 yrs (2011) → 4.7 yrs (2015); median life 4.9 yrs, some >7.7. Hindenburg 2019 alleged 58.3%→51% fade in 25 months; warranties ~95% output / ~52% efficiency.
  • Service gross margin inflected: Q4'25 +16.9% (vs −1.7% yr earlier), 6th straight profitable quarter — the annuity is starting to pay.
  • _Non-public:_ actual current-density set-points, per-vintage fade curves, stack-replacement cost per MWh.
  • Sources: ACS – thermal cycling · Bloom – longevity · Hindenburg 2019

Q10 · What if FEOC / material-assistance compliance fails?

Verdict (med): The regime is now concrete (IRS Notice 2026-15, Feb 2026) and governs the §48E credit Bloom's customers rely on. Bloom's near-fully-domestic US manufacturing (Fremont, Newark) positions it comparatively well, but FEOC-vulnerable inputs (scandia/yttria-stabilized zirconia electrolyte, nickel, power electronics — often China-linked) could breach the cost-ratio test and strip a customer's 30% ITC.

  • OBBBA added FEOC/"material assistance" cost-ratio tests to §48E/§45Y/§45X, effective tax years after 2025. Notice 2026-15 gives interim safe harbors; broader PFE-status guidance deferred (long-tail audit risk).
  • _Non-public (load-bearing):_ Bloom's exact bill-of-materials sourcing and its measured material-assistance cost ratio — the unknown that decides customer-ITC survival.
  • Sources: IRS – material assistance guidance · Novogradac – Notice 2026-15

Q2 · Will biogas and hydrogen ever scale for Bloom?

Verdict (med-high): Unlikely to shift the mix materially this decade. The fleet is ~91% natural gas, the AI boom pulls Bloom further toward bankable gas baseload, and both alternatives face ceilings — RNG supply is small and transport-dominated, and green hydrogen's §45V lifeline now needs construction start before Jan 1, 2028.

  • N. American RNG ~71.5M MMBtu (2022) → ~160M by 2030, mostly for transport. §45V up to $3/kg (LCOH >$4 → <$1.50) but ~52 clean-H₂ projects cancelled in 18 months to Sept 2025.
  • No disclosed quantified 2030 fuel-mix target; demand signal is all gas ($5B Brookfield, $7.65B of DC contracts in a 90-day window).
  • Sources: cCarbon – RNG outlook · 45V 2028 deadline

Q5 · How durable is the 30% ITC after the 2033 phase-out?

Verdict (high on schedule): Under OBBBA (July 2025), §48E(j) gives fuel cells a flat 30% rate (no prevailing-wage, no adders, no emissions test), but the credit steps down by construction-start year: 100% through 2033, 75% (2034), 50% (2035), 0% (2036+). So it's durable and generous through end-2033, then sunsets. Given wind/solar precedent of constant revision, treat 2034+ as soft — but that tail sits far outside a 2026–2028 base case.

  • Transferability (§6418) preserved; FEOC/material-assistance rules apply. Bloom claims ITC at placed-in-service/COD, not construction-start; backlog value "reflects anticipated ITC."
  • Lobbying: FCHEA is lead advocate (helped restore §45V + fuel-cell ITC in OBBBA). Note: the "ITC expiring/Congressional inaction" alarm circulating online is dated July 2024, pre-OBBBA — not the current state.
  • Sources: Project Finance – §48E fuel cells · RSM – OBBBA clean energy · ITC/PTC history

Q3 · When must Bloom break ground on a new factory beyond 5 GW?

Verdict (high): Not near-term — and the premise needs reframing. ~5 GW is not Bloom's current output; it's the expandable ceiling of the existing Fremont + Newark buildings under "copy exact." Run-rate is ~1 GW today → ~2 GW by end-2026, with headroom to ~5 GW inside existing shells at ~$100M per incremental GW. A greenfield factory is only forced if demand sustainably exceeds ~5 GW — plausibly a 2028+ question.

  • Math: 5 GW × ~$3,000–4,000/kW ≈ $15–20B of product revenue — the existing footprint alone can roughly service the entire ~$20B backlog. 2026 is delivery/COD-paced, not capacity-constrained (guide $3.4–3.8B).
  • "Copy exact" replication ~12–18 months; a new building would be US-sited for FEOC/IRA reasons. No new-factory site/timing announced as of Q1 2026.
  • _Non-public:_ exact ASP, current utilization, per-site COD schedule.
  • Sources: Utility Dive – 2 GW capacity · Fremont factory opening

What this changes for the thesis

The bull case hinges on the turbine bottleneck (Q1) and grid/nuclear lag (Q4) keeping speed-to-power valuable long enough for the native 800V DC edge (Q11) to convert into durable backlog — with the 2028–2030 window as the key bear pivot when turbine supply, SMR pilots, and AC-to-800VDC sidecars could erode Bloom's advantages at once. The most durable open risk is revenue quality (Q9): ~half of sales route through JVs Bloom finances, and the related-vs-third-party margin split is simply not disclosed.

05 Questions/Open Questions \u2014 Answe.title: Open Questions — Answered (research pass, July 2026)
05 Questions/Open Questions \u2014 Answe.html:
Open Questions — Answered (research pass, July 2026)

Follow-up pass on the four questions opened by the June 30 Brookfield expansion and the July 8 Hunterbrook short report. Format matches the June synthesis: verdict, evidence, what stays non-public, sources.

Q1 · Is Bloom's scandium supply really China-dependent, and does the scarcity math hold?

Verdict (med — genuinely contested): Both sides are partially right. Hunterbrook's current-market facts are stronger than Bloom's denial suggests; Bloom's potential-supply argument is stronger than Hunterbrook's scarcity math suggests.

  • The scarcity numbers: independent data (INN/market sources) put current global scandium oxide production at only ~25–40 t/yr against ~117 t/yr projected demand — so at today's production, even Hunterbrook's "240 t projected supply" is generous. If 5 GW really needs ~220 t/yr, supply must scale several-fold regardless of geography. Bloom's counter: scandium is an economics problem, not an availability problem — it's more abundant in the crust than lead, and titanium-processing waste streams (10M t of ore/yr, >half processed outside China) could yield "several hundred tons" annually via Bloom's proprietary tailings-recovery processes.
  • The China question: Hunterbrook's most concrete claims — a Hunan Oriental Scandium sales rep calling itself "BE's largest supplier" (Hunan claims >50% of global fuel-cell-grade share), and trade data showing Hong Kong Three Circle Electronic shipping "ceramic substrate for fuel cell application" to Bloom in 2024–25 — were not specifically refuted. Bloom's blog (posted Jul 7, updated Jul 8 — the day of the report) says it sources from "multiple sources and suppliers across multiple countries," is "not dependent on China," and keeps sourcing confidential. Note the careful wording: not dependent ≠ not sourcing from China today.
  • New wrinkle: a second short attack ("Crossroads Capital") followed Hunterbrook; Bloom denied again and the stock closed higher.
  • _Non-public (load-bearing):_ Bloom's actual supplier list and volumes; whether Chinese material is in the current mix even if substitutable. Note this also feeds the FEOC/§48E material-assistance question (June Q10) — if Chinese scandium is in the BOM, it's not just a supply risk, it's a customer-ITC risk.
  • For the presentation: frame as "management assertion vs short-seller trade data — the truth is confidential; the exposure is real either way because supply must scale 5–10× for the growth plan."

Q2 · How real is the "25 GW/yr" supply-chain visibility claim?

Verdict (med-high): It's a scandium supply-chain claim, not a factory-capacity claim — and it first appeared in Bloom's defensive blog/8-K. The COO blog says the "current diversified global supply chain of scandium oxide can support up to 25 GW per year of production capacity." Factory reality is unchanged: ~1 GW run-rate → 2 GW end-2026 → ~5 GW ceiling in existing shells (June Q3 answer stands). So 25 GW describes raw-material headroom, deployed rhetorically to counter the scarcity narrative. Don't cite it as capacity guidance.

Q3 · How does the $25B Brookfield framework actually work?

Verdict (med on structure, high on implication): Still a financing framework, not orders. Public detail is thin: funds come from Brookfield's AI Infrastructure Fund (Nov 2025, $100B target) to "finance power projects for AI infrastructure" using Bloom systems — project financing, not general corporate lending. No disclosed take-or-pay, GW commitment, or JV economics for the expansion. The original $5B deal ran through JVs Bloom co-owns (equity-method, cost-plus pricing, margin split undisclosed) — no indication the mechanics changed, so the June Q9 revenue-quality caveat now applies to a 5× larger channel. Analysts also flagged the optics: coverage noted a ~40:1 backlog-to-RPO ratio in the wake of the short report.

  • _Non-public:_ JV equity splits, per-project offtake terms, whether Bloom co-invests in the expanded framework.

Q4 · RPO: $441M or $492M? (verification)

Verdict (resolved, high): Both numbers are correct — different scopes. Q1 2026 10-Q: $441.1M product + installation RPO plus $51.5M service RPO = $492.6M total as of Mar 31, 2026. Hunterbrook quotes the total; our site quotes product+install. Action: clarify the site copy to "~$441M of firm product orders (~$493M including service)" so it can't be called stale or cherry-picked.

What this changes for the thesis

The short report doesn't introduce a new risk — it weaponizes two we already carry (backlog-vs-RPO, input concentration) with fresh trade-data specifics. The Brookfield expansion cuts both ways and now dominates both the bull case (demand + capital validation) and the bear case (5× more revenue routed through a financed, undisclosed-margin channel). Scandium graduates from a footnote (FEOC) to a named, dated risk with a live disclosure fight. Net: thesis unchanged — "real franchise, unforgiving price" — but the risk section is now the most current part of the pitch.

Sources

  • Bloom COO blog (Jul 7–8, 2026): https://www.bloomenergy.com/blog/demystifying-scandium-oxide-why-it-matters-in-bloom-fuel-cells/
  • Hunterbrook, "Bloom's Big Lie" (Jul 8, 2026): https://newsletter.hntrbrk.com/p/blooms-big-lie
  • Bloom 8-K rebuttal (Jul 9, 2026): https://www.stocktitan.net/sec-filings/BE/8-k-bloom-energy-corp-reports-material-event-fcbd2d56d6b6.html
  • Scandium market data: https://investingnews.com/daily/resource-investing/critical-metals-investing/scandium-production-the-problem-and-the-opportunity/
  • Rio Tinto Sorel-Tracy expansion (9 t/yr): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/863064/000086306425000023/form6k2025nov.htm

03 Drafts/Section 4.7 - Tax Credit Stack.title: 4.7 The tax credit stack: fuel cells were the OBBBA winner
03 Drafts/Section 4.7 - Tax Credit Stack.html:
4.7 The tax credit stack: fuel cells were the OBBBA winner

Source: P.L. 119-21 (OBBBA), Treasury/IRS guidance, Crux 2025 H2 market report

In 2022, the Biden administration passed the Inflation Reduction Act, which handed clean energy companies hundreds of billions in tax breaks. The big question heading into 2025 was whether the second Trump administration would rip those credits up. For fuel cells, the answer came in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed July 4, 2025, and Bloom came out ahead. The headline rule for Bloom is the federal Investment Tax Credit, which lets a buyer (or a tax-equity partner) deduct 30% of the cost of every fuel cell system they install straight off their federal tax bill. Wind and solar had to clear extra hoops to keep that 30%, like paying union-scale wages and proving zero emissions. Fuel cells were carved out and skip both, so even Bloom's natural-gas units qualify for the full 30%. The credit also lasts much longer than it does for wind or solar: 100% of the credit is available through 2033, then it tapers (75% in 2034, 50% in 2035, gone in 2036). Wind and solar by contrast have to break ground by July 4, 2026, or get nothing. JPMorgan upgraded BE and raised its FY26 EBITDA estimate to ~$420M after the bill passed. The catch: bonus credits for building in coal-country zip codes or using American-made parts were not extended to fuel cells, so 30% is the ceiling.

Three other credits round out the stack. First, customers who use Bloom's reverse-mode units to make hydrogen can claim up to $3 per kilogram produced, though the construction deadline was pulled forward to 1/1/2028. Bloom's SOEC unit hits ~80% efficiency with waste heat and is best positioned to hit the top tier. Second, the carbon capture credit pays $85/t industrial CO₂ or $180/t DAC, and Bloom's Chart partnership monetizes this directly because SOFC exhaust is 10× more concentrated. Third, and importantly for Bloom's business model, companies that can't use credits themselves can sell them for ~93¢ on the dollar in a market that grew 48% YoY to $42B. State rebates layer on top, most notably California's SGIP program ($0.60/W for >1 MW fuel cells).

Bottom line: the 30% federal credit translates to ~$2,250/kW capex relief, or ~3¢/kWh levelized, which closes the gap between unsubsidized $0.13-0.16/kWh and the ~$0.10/kWh commercial grid average. The real 2026 risk is FEOC / 'material-assistance' foreign-entity rules effective 1/1/2026, which apply to fuel-cell components and put supply-chain compliance in the analyst's crosshairs for 2026-2027.

Revenue Driver Tree — the forecast architecture

Companion analysis to the Annotated Fact Base. This is the skeleton the forecast hangs on: the revenue engine that later feeds valuation (WACC/DCF out of scope here). Academic use; not investment advice.

The central design choice

Revenue splits at the top into two channels that grow on different logic and are modeled as separate stacks, then summed:

Revenue = Arm's-length (third-party) + Affiliate (Brookfield Fund-JV)

This split matters because the reported Q1 2026 headline of ~130% growth collapses to ~17% once the affiliate channel is removed. The Q1 2026 split: $377.8M arm's-length (+16.9% YoY) vs $373.3M affiliate [D]/[R]. Mashing them together is exactly how a 130% quarter fools you into reading a 17% one.

01 — Product, arm's-length: the engine

The arm's-length Product line is where the business compounds. It is a volume × price node:

  • Product = MW added × ASP
  • MW added = min(Bookings, Capacity)
  • Bookings = Serviceable market × Win-rate

Near term the binding constraint is capacity (demand runs well past the ~1 GW line), so the schedule to 2 GW by YE2026, expandable to 5 GW, sets the ceiling [R]/[E]. Later, as capacity catches up, bookings become the constraint — the driver that matters flips partway through the horizon. Calibrate ASP to historical Product revenue ÷ implied MW shipped, then trend it. Base/bull/bear are driven by win-rate and ASP, not an invented growth rate.

02 — Installation, Service, Electricity

  • Installation — the loss-leader. Priced to win the box, not to make money; gross margin has been negative every year on record. Modeled as an attach onto Product MW: Installation = Attach% × Product_MW × Install_$/MW. Hold margin in its historical negative band.
  • Service — the durable floor. The annuity and lowest-variance line. Driven by cumulative deployed capacity, not current-year sales: Cum_GW = prior Cum_GW + MW_added; Service = Cum_GW × Service_$/GW·yr. Factor ≈ $127M per GW-year (FY2025 service revenue / cumulative GW) [D]; margin ≈ 13% [R].
  • Electricity — the run-off. Legacy own-and-operate PPA fleet, deliberately shrinking. A decay line, not a growth line; don't extrapolate its margin swings (one PPA write-down drove a −117% year).

03 — The affiliate channel, and its drag

Product sold into the Brookfield Fund-JVs is real revenue, but it is gated by how fast Brookfield deploys capital under the $5B / five-year framework — of which only a small fraction is funded so far (~$11.4M committed-but-unfunded at Q1 2026) [R]. It is a scenario switch, not a trend: do not assume the full $5B converts on a smooth curve.

Two contra-effects ride with it, both scaling with affiliate shipments (not arm's-length sales): the equity-method loss Bloom books on the JVs' losses, and the deferred profit it cannot recognize while it part-owns the buyer. On the tree these are the dashed contra — a deduction from the affiliate channel's net contribution, not from consolidated revenue.

04 — The four assumptions that move the answer

A driver tree is only as honest as the handful of inputs the valuation turns on:

1. Win-rate and 2. ASP trajectory set the arm's-length top line.

3. Brookfield deployment pace sets the size and durability of the affiliate channel (the primary base/bull/bear lever).

4. Terminal margin — must be defended against the segment table, since blended margin only improves because mix keeps tilting toward Product.

Capacity factor and gas price sit in the cost work but feed the win-rate link (delivered cost) and the Electricity segment.

Key anchors used here

Capacity ~1 GW today → 2 GW by YE2026, expandable to 5 GW [R]/[E]; Brookfield $5B/5-yr framework, small funded fraction (~$11.4M unfunded at Q1 2026) [R]; segment gross margins (Product ~35%, Installation negative, Service ~13% FY2025, Electricity volatile) [R]; Q1 2026 split $377.8M arm's-length (+16.9%) vs $373.3M affiliate [D]/[R]; service annuity ≈ $127M per GW-year [D]. Tiers: [R] reported, [D] derived, [E] estimate.

03 Drafts/Section 2.5 - Business Moat.title: 2.5 The business moat: why no SOFC competitor can catch up at gigawatt scale
03 Drafts/Section 2.5 - Business Moat.html:
2.5 The business moat: why no SOFC competitor can catch up at gigawatt scale

Source: Bloom FY2025 10-K, competitor filings, third-party analysis

The chemistry edge in section 2 explains why SOFC beats PEM and MCFC on paper. It does not explain why no other SOFC maker has caught up. The honest answer is that Bloom has been building the factory and putting boxes in the ground since 2008, while every credible Western competitor is still measured in pilots. As of YE 2025, Bloom had ~1.8 GW cumulatively deployed across 1,200+ sites in nine countries, generating $2.02B of FY2025 revenue (+37% YoY) on a $6B product backlog (2.5× YoY) and ~$20B total backlog. The nearest Western SOFC peers are an order of magnitude smaller. Doosan Fuel Cell only began SOFC mass production in July 2025 at 50 MW/year using Ceres-licensed tech. Bosch exited SOFC entirely in February 2025 and laid off 550 staff. Mitsubishi's MEGAMIE is still a 250 kW Japan-only product. Bloom's Fremont line is at 1 GW/year today, on track for 2 GW by YE 2026, expandable to 5 GW, roughly 20× any single Western competitor. The deal sheet matches the factory: Brookfield $5B / 1 GW for behind-the-meter AI power, AEP up to 1 GW / ~$2.65B, Oracle 2.8 GW, SK ecoplant 500 MW / $4.5B, Equinix 100+ MW, and Nebius $2.6B / 250 MW guaranteed.

The thread: technology gets a competitor in the room, but a decade of installed base, a manufacturing footprint, and customer concentration in the hyperscalers actually buying gigawatts today is the real moat. The honest counter: top three customers were 53% of FY2024 revenue (#1 is the SK related party). Bloom has carried a ~$4.0B accumulated deficit and has never sustainably earned its cost of capital. And on a gas feedstock, lifecycle emissions are roughly 679–839 lb CO₂/MWh, at parity with a modern gas turbine, not zero. The moat is the head start, not the molecule.

03 Drafts/Efficiency Fuel and R&D Pipeli.title: Efficiency, Fuel, and the R&D Pipeline
03 Drafts/Efficiency Fuel and R&D Pipeli.html:
Efficiency, Fuel, and the R&D Pipeline

Companion analysis to the Annotated Fact Base — answers to the open questions on efficiency, the fuel bill, the 800°C heat, and R&D. Academic use; not investment advice.

01 — What the efficiency numbers mean

η = electricity out ÷ fuel energy in; gas per MWh = 3.412 MMBtu ÷ η. Three numbers circulate and measure different things:

| Figure | What it measures |

|---|---|

| 54% | Average lifetime electrical efficiency on natural gas, degradation included |

| 60% | Electrical efficiency on 100% hydrogen at the R&D facility |

| 90% | Combined electricity plus captured, used heat (CHP) — not applicable to data centers |

The trap is stacking the 90% against the 54%. The 90% is not electrical efficiency — it counts heat recovered only when something next door uses it. On gas with the heat vented, the box is a 54% machine. (Note: the site's "53–65%" is the Server 6.5 datasheet nameplate range; 54% is the honest lifetime, real-world figure.)

02 — The fuel bill, and whether the boxes need a gas line

Yes — every box needs a gas line. "Off-grid" means off the electric grid, not off fuel. The customer pays a gas utility every month, on top of buying the box and paying Bloom to service it.

At 54%: 3.412 ÷ 0.54 = 6.32 MMBtu of gas per MWh.

| Natural gas price | Fuel $/MWh @ 54% | Fuel $/MWh @ 40% genset | Efficiency saving |

|---|---|---|---|

| $3.50/MMBtu (Henry Hub, mid-2026) | $22 | $30 | $8 |

| $5.00/MMBtu (delivered, incl. transport) | $32 | $43 | $11 |

Project Jupiter (Oracle) makes the fuel bill physical: up to 2.45 GW fed by a fast-tracked 16-mile, 400 MMcf/day pipeline. 2.45 GW at 54% needs ~372,000 MMBtu/day against the line's ~414,000.

| Project Jupiter | Figure |

|---|---|

| Bloom capacity | up to 2.45 GW [R] |

| Gas throughput | up to 400 MMcf/day ≈ 151M MMBtu/yr [R]/[D] |

| Annual gas bill | ~$530M ($3.50) to ~$760M ($5.00) [D] |

| One-time equipment | ~$18B (2.45 GW × ~$7,500/kW) [D] |

| Fuel saved vs 40% genset | ~$185M–$265M / yr [D] |

| Cumulative fuel, 15–20 yr | ~$8B–$15B [D] |

The key takeaway: fuel is only $22–32 of Bloom's ~$121/MWh all-in cost (≈$95 with the 30% ITC). The box and stack-replacement service are the other four-fifths. Higher efficiency shrinks only the fuel slice — which is why Bloom undercuts an inefficient on-site genset on fuel yet still costs more than grid power overall. Hydrogen makes the fuel line ~10× worse (~$200/MWh at $4–6/kg), which is why no site runs on it yet.

03 — Capturing the 800°C heat

The module runs ~800°C internally and exhausts heat averaging >350°C — high-grade where a PEM cell's sub-100°C heat is not. Captured and used, this heat is the only thing that turns the 54% electrical figure into the 90% combined one. The data-center use that matters is cooling: routing exhaust through an absorption chiller displaces electric chillers, with management claiming a 10–15% PUE gain. Caveats: the chiller is added capital that only pays where a thermal load sits next to the box; Bloom's heat figures are modeled to ±5%, not field-validated; >350°C is well short of turbine-grade. Treat it as a win-rate input, not a revenue line.

04 — R&D spend

Reported only in aggregate (no project-level breakout). Per-project budgets quoted in third-party summaries are invented.

| Period | R&D ($000s) | R&D % of revenue |

|---|---|---|

| FY2021 | 103,396 | 10.6% |

| FY2022 | 150,606 | 12.6% |

| FY2023 | 155,865 | 11.7% |

| FY2024 | 148,629 | 10.1% |

| FY2025 | 185,993 | 9.2% |

| Q1 2026 | 56,849 | 7.6% |

Dollars rose ~80% (FY21→FY25) while falling from 10.6% to 7.6% of revenue — operating leverage on a commercialized platform, not rising research intensity. Model R&D as a declining % of revenue.

05 — Technology directions (disclosed)

  • Capacity scale-up — ~1 GW today; 2 GW by end-2026, expandable to 5 GW; ~$100M for the next GW [R]/[E].
  • Native 800V DC output — all new shipments DC-ready; aligns with NVIDIA's 2027 800V DC racks [R]/[E].
  • SOEC electrolyzer (hydrogen) — same platform in reverse; ~2 GW/yr line at Newark since 2022, INL-validated as most efficient commercial-scale electrolyzer measured. Gated by the $3/kg H₂ credit requiring construction start by Jan 2028 [R].
  • Carbon capture (CCUS) — concentrated exhaust makes capture cheaper than a turbine's; Chart Industries JV targets the $85/ton credit [R].

Both hydrogen and carbon are subsidy-linked terminal-value optionality, not near-term drivers.

06 — Claims to keep OUT of the model (corrections)

Three external/AI summaries introduced items that don't survive sourcing:

  • No Bloom "Khyber/Rubin" or "Direct-Conversion Architectures" project — Rubin and Kyber are NVIDIA's.
  • The "10× capacity, constant headcount" line traces to nothing — the disclosed claim is 2 GW by end-2026, expandable to 5 GW.
  • "Quanta" is a customer (Quanta Computer, expanded an install 150% in Nov 2024), not a microgrid project; the flagship microgrid is Oracle's Jupiter.
  • "70–80% repeat customers" overstates management's "over two-thirds."

07 — How this maps to the driver tree

None of this is a revenue line. Efficiency, heat capture, and 800V DC feed win-rate; the gas-line requirement and fuel bill are a customer operating-cost / siting constraint; capacity scale-up feeds the MW cap; hydrogen and carbon are subsidy-linked terminal optionality. The only forecast input here is aggregate R&D, projected as a declining % of revenue.

03 Drafts/Formulas Rates and Units Key (.html:
Formulas, Rates, and Units — the key

Companion key to the Driver Tree and Fact Base: every equation and $/unit notation used across the documents, written out. Tiers: [R] reported, [D] derived, [E] estimate.

Formulas (the driver tree + efficiency math)

| Formula | Meaning / typical value |

|---|---|

| Revenue = Arm's-length + Affiliate | Top of the tree; the two channels are forecast separately and summed. Reported Q1'26 growth of 130% is ~17% arm's-length [D]. |

| Product = MW × ASP | The arm's-length engine; the core volume × price node. |

| MW added = min(Bookings, Capacity) | Binding constraint flips: capacity-limited near term, demand-limited later. |

| Bookings = Serviceable market × Win-rate | Feeds MW added once capacity is no longer the cap. |

| Installation = Attach% × Product_MW × Install_$/MW | Loss-leader tied to product volume; held at historical negative margin. |

| Cum.GW = prior Cum.GW + MW added | Cumulative installed base; drives the Service annuity. |

| Service = Cum.GW × Service_$/GW·yr | Durable recurring line; factor ≈ $127M/GW-yr [D]. |

| Affiliate = JV_MW × ASP | Gated by Brookfield's deployment pace; a scenario switch, not a trend. |

| ASP ≈ Product revenue ÷ implied MW | Grounds the ASP input before it is trended. |

| η = electricity out ÷ fuel energy in | Electrical efficiency: 54% on natural gas [R]. |

| Gas per MWh = 3.412 MMBtu ÷ η | At 54%: 6.32 MMBtu/MWh [D]. |

Rates and units

| Unit | How it applies / typical value |

|---|---|

| $/kW | Equipment ≈ $7,500/kW [E]; 30% ITC ≈ $2,250/kW [D]; ASP quoted per kW. |

| $/MWh | All-in delivered cost ≈ $121/MWh (≈ $95 with 30% ITC) [D]; fuel slice $22–32/MWh [D]. |

| $/GW-yr | Service annuity factor ≈ $127M/GW-yr [D]. |

| $/MMBtu | Henry Hub ≈ $3.50; delivered ≈ $5.00 [E]. |

| $/kg | H₂ fuel ≈ $4–6/kg (≈$200/MWh) [E]; $3/kg production credit [R]. |

| $/ton | Carbon-capture credit $85/ton ($180/ton if pulled from air) [R]. |

| $/W | California SGIP rebate ≈ $0.60/W of fuel-cell capacity [R]. |

| % of revenue | Capex 3.5% of revenue [R]; gross margins (Product ≈35%, Service ≈13%). |

| MWh / MMBtu | 1 MWh = 3.412 MMBtu [R]; 6.32 MMBtu makes 1 MWh at 54% [D]. |

| MMcf/day | Pipeline throughput; the Jupiter line moves up to 400 MMcf/day ≈ 151M MMBtu/yr [R]/[D]. |

Related shorthand

  • ASP — average selling price, the Product node price, quoted in $/kW.
  • LCOE — levelized cost of energy; the all-in $/MWh metric (≈$121/MWh unsubsidized) [D].
  • ITC — 30% Investment Tax Credit on installed cost; trims delivered cost ≈$26/MWh [D].
  • RPO — remaining performance obligations; contracted unbilled backlog (Product $441.1M at Q1'26) [R].
  • PUE — power usage effectiveness; heat capture claims a 10–15% gain.
  • CHP — combined heat and power; electricity plus captured, used heat (the 90% combined figure).

All-in cost, fuel, and credit values swing most on gas price, capacity factor, and the efficiency assumption. Academic use; not investment advice.

03 Drafts/Section 06 - Bear Thesis.title: 06 The bear thesis: what could go wrong
03 Drafts/Section 06 - Bear Thesis.html:
06 The bear thesis: what could go wrong

Source: Hindenburg Research, Bloom 10-K filings, short interest data

A balanced read has to acknowledge the other side. The most prominent bear case is Hindenburg Research's September 2019 short report, which argued the Energy Server's economics were materially worse than disclosed: real-world efficiency degraded from 58.3% at install to ~51% after 25 months, and average stack life was 4.8 to 5.2 years against >5-year warranties, meaning service costs were structurally underreserved. Bloom disputed the report, but the underlying physics (ceramic stacks degrade with use and must be replaced) is the same "double-edged sword" already flagged in section 1.

The financials add fuel. Bloom carries a ~$4.0B accumulated deficit and has never sustainably earned its cost of capital after ~17 years. Share count is up roughly 23% over the past year between two large convertibles, so equity holders are absorbing real dilution as the company scales. Top three customers were 53% of FY2024 revenue with #1 being a related party. And valuation is rich: BE trades at ~80× book value and an EV/EBITDA above 600×, with short interest near 10% of float as of January 2026. The bull case requires backlog conversion, lasting subsidies, manageable degradation, and easing concentration. None of those are facts yet. Worth flagging now, worth a deeper look later.
